ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03302050 represents wheat noodle food product as the closest parent; no exact code for plain boiled wheat noodle without sauce exists in FoodOn as of 2026-04.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers cooked pasta and noodles as the closest parent category; no dedicated code for plain Chinese wheat noodle exists.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226529007 represents noodles as a food concept; used as closest parent as no plain boiled noodle specific code is available.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical restaurant serving of plain noodles is approximately 200 g, equivalent to a standard bowl as served at Din Tai Fung and similar Chinese restaurants in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Noodles, Chinese, plain, cooked — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB No. 20113)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for cooked Chinese-style plain wheat noodles used as the primary nutrient reference. Values represent plain boiled wheat noodles without sauce or seasoning, consistent with the dish as served. Sodium adjusted to reflect minimal salt in cooking water per standard preparation; USDA value retained as a reasonable estimate.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 52 is derived from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for white wheat noodles, boiled (Chinese-style), which is the closest analogous food to plain boiled wheat noodles. Reference: Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (52 × 54.8) / 100 ≈ 28.5, based on 200 g serving containing approximately 54.8 g carbohydrate.

hypertension.guidance: Plain boiled noodles without sauce contain approximately 5 mg sodium per 100 g, making them a low-sodium staple suitable for hypertensive individuals, provided high-sodium sauces or broths are not added at serving.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index and high carbohydrate density
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 52 and approximately 27.4 g carbohydrate per 100 g, plain noodles have a moderate glycemic impact; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor portion size and pair with protein or non-starchy vegetables to blunt postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low saturated fat and negligible cholesterol
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Plain boiled wheat noodles contain only 0.1 g saturated fat and negligible cholesterol per 100 g, making them a heart-friendly carbohydrate base when consumed without high-fat or high-sodium accompaniments.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Plain noodle (光面, Guāng Miàn) is a minimally processed Chinese wheat noodle dish consisting of boiled wheat-flour noodles served without sauce, broth, or toppings. The name literally means 'bare noodle' or 'plain noodle' in Mandarin Chinese. In Singapore, plain noodles are a common staple side dish served at Chinese restaurants, hawker centres, and dim sum establishments such as Din Tai Fung, where they are offered as a neutral accompaniment to braised meats, dumplings, or other flavourful dishes. The noodles are typically made from wheat flour, water, and a small amount of salt, then boiled until tender and drained. The texture is soft and slightly chewy, with a mild, neutral wheat flavour that readily absorbs accompanying sauces. Nutritionally, plain boiled wheat noodles provide approximately 138 kcal per 100 g, with 27.4 g carbohydrate, 4.5 g protein, and only 0.6 g fat, making them a low-fat, moderate-carbohydrate staple. Dietary fibre content is modest at 1.2 g per 100 g. Sodium is very low at approximately 5 mg per 100 g when prepared without added salt. The glycemic index is approximately 52 (low-medium range), and the glycemic load per standard 200 g serving is approximately 28.5, which is high due to portion size. Clinically, plain noodles are suitable for hypertensive individuals due to their low sodium content, but individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor portion size and pair with protein or fibre-rich foods to moderate postprandial glycaemia. The dish is vegetarian, vegan, and halal-friendly, and contains gluten as a primary allergen.
common_user_queries: How many calories in plain noodles?
Is plain noodle good for diabetics?
Din Tai Fung plain noodle calories

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy for cooked Chinese-style plain wheat noodles, which is the closest approved source match. No exact Singapore-specific HPB Foodo entry for plain boiled wheat noodle without sauce was identified. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for white wheat noodles, boiled. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the adapted rather than exact source match.
notes: 1. Nutrient values sourced from USDA SR Legacy (NDB No. 20113, cooked Chinese-style plain noodles) as the closest approved source; HPB Singapore Foodo does not carry a dedicated entry for plain boiled wheat noodle without sauce or seasoning as of 2026-04. 2. Sodium value of 5 mg per 100 g reflects plain boiled noodles with minimal salt; restaurant preparations may vary. 3. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for this entry. 4. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for this entry. 5. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for this entry. 6. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA source; plain wheat noodles contain no trans fats by formulation. 7. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero; plain wheat noodles contain no animal-derived ingredients. 8. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero; plain wheat noodles contain no vitamin C. 9. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero; plain wheat noodles are plant-based and contain no vitamin B12. 10. GI of 52 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for white wheat noodles, boiled (Chinese-style); no exact GI study for this specific product was identified. 11. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (52 × 54.8 g carbohydrate in 200 g serving) / 100 ≈ 28.5. 12. FoodOn, FoodEx2, and SNOMED CT codes are closest-parent matches; no exact ontology code for plain boiled Chinese wheat noodle without sauce exists in these ontologies as of 2026-04. 13. The dish is classified as Chinese cuisine and served in Singapore; multilingual names are provided for English, Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi per Singaporean dish requirements.
